mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses philosophy of art, diverse conceptions of art's scope, and theories about art's nature and functions (A.1, A.2).
Covers aesthetic theories including formalist and expressive approaches to art, beauty, and taste -- central to diverse theories of art's nature (A.2).
Examines philosophical theories of beauty and taste in art, relevant to mimetic, formalist, and expressive art theories (A.2) and the distinction between fine and useful art (B.1.a).
Directly addresses the creative process and creative expression in art-making (A.3).
Explores imagination's role in artistic creation and aesthetic experience, directly relevant to the creative process (A.3).
Addresses classification and periodization of arts and style analysis, relevant to classification of the arts (B) and varieties of style (B.4).
Explores the distinction between useful and fine art, and form versus function in art classification (B.1.a).
Addresses mimetic, expressive, formalist, and pragmatic theories as applied to literature as an art form (A.2).
Examines current debates about what qualifies as art and how contemporary works challenge traditional classifications (A.1, B.1).
Covers how modernist movements redefined conceptions of art and created new stylistic categories (A.1, B.4).
Explores avant-garde movements that challenged boundaries between art kinds and conventional style categories (B.1, B.4).
Directly relevant to characterizing folk and popular arts by their audience and cultural context (B.3).
Studies music across cultural contexts, relevant to classifying arts by social circumstances of production and audience character (B.3).
Covers performers as interpreters and creators of art, and the use of notational devices in theatrical arts (B.1.c, B.1.d).
Addresses music as art classified by notation (B.1.d), performers as interpreters/creators (B.1.c), and the distinction between production and consumption.
Exemplifies a major period style across literature, music, and visual arts, directly relevant to varieties of style (B.4.b).
Exemplifies period style across multiple art forms, relevant to the discussion of period, school, and personal styles (B.4.b).
Addresses postmodernist theories challenging traditional conceptions of art's nature and function, and represents a period style (A.2, B.4.b).
Exemplifies school styles that challenged prevailing theories of art's nature and the boundaries between art kinds (A.2, B.4.b).
Provides frameworks for understanding primitive, folk, and popular arts characterized by cultural/social context (B.3).
Frankfurt School approaches address pragmatic theories of art's social functions and effects (A.2).
Covers aesthetics and German idealist art theory, a major tradition in the philosophy of art (A.1, A.2).
Illustrates classification of arts by physical matter and the production of artistic works as physical objects (B.1.b), and period/regional styles (B.4.b).
